When you look at like Cursor or Copilot, I think where they often struggle, and I think Cursor did this relatively strategically, is that if you are trying to index a large code base, you run into a lot of the capacity issues that are innate to the background agents you're building on.
So for example, agents, they're moving code back and forth in these not necessarily simple fragments of code, they're called tokens, and those tokens are capped by certain sizes.

So for Claude for a long time, it was 250,000 tokens, which sounds like a lot, but if you have an enormous code base that you need the agent to learn about before it can act, you can exhaust that token count really quickly.

And so what Cursor would do is like, it would have people focus in on specific sets of files, or like, and we do the same thing, where it'll say something like, let's just focus on the workspace, which just might be a folder that you have checked.
So it's not going to be the full scan of it.
It's just going to be like whatever snippet you have locally.
And so they did that to kind of save on these token issues.
And so we handle it a little bit differently.
And so we can grok a little bit more of your code base, not just from the files that are local, but also what files they're looking at, like what the comments are.
And then we do some other like proprietary things to kind of tweak how we deal with the token sizes.
So we can get a little deeper in the code base than some of our competitors can.
Now, all that being said, all this is changing day in and day out.
so things like Gemini, for example, can they allow like a 10x bigger code base set than everybody else.

So like now Cursor does fast and slow prompts, but it's just the idea is like they're going to give you so many of each model's time with your license, and then you have to be very judicious with doing it.
But because we didn't do that, people were like, oh, at the time we were doing unlimited like chat calls, if you paid a single fee, we were basically unlimited.

We were $40 a month, and we had no caps.
And so people were like, oh, well, here's a top model wrapper at relatively a little bit more money, but an unlimited usage fee.
And so I would say those were kind of the three big ways.

So I think right now we use the size of the engineering team as a proxy for code complexity.
And so a lot of times if you have that many engineers, you probably have, you know, multiple repositories of code, like an older, maybe your company has been around for 15, 20 years, right?
And so you have an older repository that maybe supports part of your code base or part of your tools or part of your hardware.

And then you have a more modern tool base that, you know, that maybe is more cloud-focused that you're trying to, like, modernize and you're trying to bring tools from the old code base into this modern code base.
And in some cases, people often have even a third code base.
That might be like their next, next generation or like where they're going afterward or may fall back.
Like if this cloud vendor were to disappear, they would fall back to another cloud vendor or whatever it is.
And so it's this movement between this complexity is a lot of times where we find our sweet spot, right?
And so I think that's kind of where the sales team lands.
I think the other thing we've seen substantial growth is what we would call self-serve, which are people who are signing up in the wild.
And that's where, I mean, these could be teams of one, they could be teams of five.
We just recently added the ability to even aggregate like teams together.
But it's just the idea is like we didn't until really until about March, we didn't really think about individual contributors as single person coders.
And so I think that's one of the areas we've seen significant growth.
But I think it's still people who...
Have a relatively complex code base.

Like, these are people who don't really have an understanding of how code works.
And they want the AI's assistant to kind of maybe get a project done and get a project off the ground.
And we definitely see those people.
They're the most price sensitive.
And so I think those are the people we've won over because our price point for unlimited usage was so low.
The other group is what I would call that kind of crosses over into our enterprise clients is, like, are your sole coders?
So these are people that have, you know, some kind of optimized workflow.
They know their IDE of choice.
And a lot of times, in this case, it's either VS Code or IntelliJ.
They've tweaked it for their own specializations.
They know what their dev environment is.
They know what their dependencies are.
They know how to run their tests.
They know what their preferred, like, terminal structure.
Like these are people who have every keyboard command memorized and they probably have an analog keyboard to match, right?
But it's just the idea is like they know how to work fast and they know how to work cleanly and we just happen to fit into that workflow for them.
So these are people who would take advantage of like our first tools.
So these are things like we call them completions, but they're like extensions autocomplete.

Cursor calls that in feature tab.

That's called next edit.
Cursor has another one that they also call tab or sometimes people call that control K or command K is the case maybe.
So that these are people that are probably they're being optimized maybe single files or maybe a single feature.
And so like that's kind of.
Distinguishes the sole coder, and they know that feature really, really, really well.
The next category up that we tend to win is what I've been calling my control coders.
These are people who understand the code base nearly completely.
They understand, like, they're looking for a specific linter, it's called, which is just like a specific style and format.
They know how to read that format really quickly.
They have to review other people's code, so they have their preferred methodologies of, like, diffing and looking at two different pieces of code side-by-side to see, like, what changes were made.
They understand how to move GitHub really quickly.
They prefer GitHub's, like, pull request model where they can quickly see changes and why people made the changes that they did.
They're making judgments on whether or not that's in a company's style.
And so that's, like, our GitHub integration makes the most sense.
To these people, because they need to have a control over an entire repo.
In some cases, they might be in charge of a public repo, and so multiple people are contributing to it, so they're actively reviewing it on the fly.
So these might be open source projects, these might be complex projects that are within a specific company.
And so they need that ability both to see individual files, to move across entire feature sets, but also look at merges really quickly.

And so that's one of the groups we've been the most successful with, because it might be that as people are moving through this complexity, they might understand a specific feature better than another.
So they might use us in our chat feature to be like, explain to me why this works the way it does, or explain to me why this style was implemented the way it was.

Or you might be a new engineer in that organization, and you're trying to learn those styles across features, and so you might say something like, explain this feature to me, or explain why this tendency exists, or why we're using Python 3.12 instead of Python.

And so I think one of the differences, and this is where things like we call the context engine really kicks in, because we take the time to try and read multiple lines and sometimes multiple files of your code base before we make a suggestion.

Where sometimes I think windsurf and cursor try and help you edit a file as quickly as possible without necessarily understanding how that file might interplay with other dependencies in your code base.

So a lot of times what we tell people is like when people are comparing these agents, the first thing they're trying to do is get a sense of whether the agents are going to work in their context.
So they might ask it to explain this code days to me or what does this file do.
We call that like first use, right?
And that if it does a good job, you get like repeat use.
so people, they might be trying to onboard to a specific code base.
They might be trying to onboard to a new style.
They might be trying to onboard to a technology the company's trying to integrate.
And that's what a lot of times people are using AI, right?
It's in these moments of uncertainty.
But it's only after these repeat uses that people can kind of habituate.
They know how to prompt.
They know what to ask the agent.
They know how small of a task to break off to get things to the AI that gives them answers that they trust, right?
So I think a lot of times what happens is people try and break off too much, and the AI will delete code, or it'll put the in the wrong spot.
And so it gives them more they have to clean up.
And so that's what breaks the staircase of trust, right?
And so what Augment got really good at early is basically saying, it's like, they memorize the tree structure of your app.

What people now do is they'll write their own markdown files, and they'll stick it in their repo to kind of trick the agent into doing that.

And so people found workarounds to mimic what Augment kind of does for you for free.

I think what the controllers want is like what they'll call deterministic outcomes.
